strands.title: Yanantin TensorRecord as Core Pipeline Protocol
strands.content: CYCLE 25 CONSOLIDATION: TensorRecord remains ground truth. No schema changes. Core insight carries forward: TensorRecord is immutable state structure with epistemic transparency, declared losses, and provenance as first-class fields. The distinction between 'log format' and 'epistemic representation' is foundational. Cycle 25 adds critical clarification from user feedback: 'If the pipeline produces more coherent cognitive trajectories than the append-only log, the pipeline is doing useful work — even if the tensor projection isn't optimal.' This reframes the confound question: tensor quality is NOT a confound, it's the hypothesis itself. If tensors are worse-than-append-only, H0 fails. If tensors are better, H0 succeeds. Either way, the result is valid.

key_claims.text: TensorRecord carries uncertainty and declared information loss as first-class state, not metadata annotation; loss is explicit and queryable, enabling the transformer to reason about its own degradation
This makes TensorRecord fundamentally different from CPU registers or memory hierarchies, where loss (cache eviction, page faults) is opaque to computation
TensorRecord is NOT a conversation log or summarization; it is a projected state representation integrating trajectory into semantic structure with epistemic transparency

Tensor quality confound is not a confound but the hypothesis itself: if tensors produce better cognitive coherence than append-only, H0 succeeds; if worse, H0 fails. Either outcome is publishable and valid.

strands.title: Dual-Tensor Pipeline Architecture with Explicit Roles
strands.content: CYCLE 25 SPECIFICATION LOCK: fetch-decode-execute-writeback cycle structure confirmed. Pipeline machinery defined in Cycle 24. Cycle 25 adds implementation entry point clarity: the controller loop is the minimal machinery needed to run the experiment. Core loop: (1) fetch tensor_durable + tensor_ephemeral from Apacheta store, (2) decode them into context for transformer, (3) execute transformer call with `<system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>` framing, (4) writeback: Haiku applies memory control (promote/evict based on rules), update tensor', store to Apacheta. This loop runs once per API round-trip. No changes to architecture. Implementation entry point: start with the controller loop skeleton, then fill in Haiku (memory rules) and tensor projection (how to compress state into TensorRecord format). Pipeline is provider-independent (works with Anthropic API + caching, OpenRouter without caching, local vLLM). Two implementations planned to validate this independence.

key_claims.text: Pipeline cycle is fetch-decode (assemble state) → execute (transformer call) → writeback (Haiku memory-control). Every user API round-trip is one cycle.

Transformer (ALU) produces tensor' including updated key_claims with epistemic metadata, strands, declared_losses, instructions_for_next. Haiku (memory controller) applies cache policy deterministically.
Pre/post-processing is not hidden work; it contributes to cycle cost and should be measured in H0 metrics (token efficiency, resource utilization)

Controller loop is minimal machinery: fetch tensors → decode to context → call transformer → writeback (Haiku promotes/evicts) → store. This loop is the implementation entry point. Provider-independent (works with Anthropic, OpenRouter, local).

strands.title: Cognitive Processing Unit Model: Epistemic Plateau as Testable Prediction
strands.content: CYCLE 25 VALIDATION STRATEGY UNCHANGED: Riemann hypothesis proof attempt is the headline task. Three conditions (append-only / epistemic projection / bounded cognition). Variance in failure modes is the measurement. Tighter embedding cluster = structured cognition; scattered cloud = incoherent cognition. Cycle 25 adds one critical refinement from user feedback: the confound (tensor quality) is not a confound, it's the research question. This simplifies the research narrative: we're not trying to isolate tensor quality from pipeline architecture. We're asking: does structured epistemic state (however imperfect) improve cognitive coherence over append-only context? If yes, publish (Outcome A). If no, publish (Outcome B). The experiment design stands. No changes to H0 hypothesis, conditions, or measurement methodology.

key_claims.text: H0 prediction: STOP signals correlate with epistemic stabilization (T > threshold, I < threshold), not context window size. Testable via Riemann proof task (guaranteed failure, variance in failure modes is measurement).

Cognitive processing unit model: bounded reasoner operates on compressed state, tracks uncertainty explicitly, recognizes epistemic plateau, declares unknowns, composes through formal semantics. Validated by task variance.

Riemann proof task isolates architecture effect: failure is guaranteed (no confound), variance in failure coherence measures cognition quality. Tighter embedding cluster = structured cognition; scattered cloud = incoherent cognition.

Three conditions (baseline / structured input / structured + explicit STOP) isolate tensor architecture's effect. Same model, same task, different input representation. Causal isolation for H0 validation.

Confound reframing (from Cycle 25): tensor quality is not a confound, it's the hypothesis. If structured epistemic state improves coherence, H0 succeeds regardless of whether tensors are perfect. Both success and failure outcomes are publishable and valid.

strands.title: Autoresearch Loop: Tensor Space Exploration (New Strand, Cycle 25)
strands.content: CYCLE 25 NEW IDEA: User suggests Karpathy's autoresearch loop (https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch) as a potential mechanism for exploring tensor space. This is distinct from the Hamutay pipeline itself, but could be a *meta-level* tool: once Hamutay can produce tensors, an autoresearch loop could systematically vary tensor parameters (strand count, claim density, epistemic thresholds) and measure which configurations produce the most coherent reasoning on benchmark tasks. Conceptual place in the research: (1) **Phase 1 (Cycles 25-32)**: Build Hamutay pipeline, run Riemann H0 experiment with fixed tensor configuration. Validate that structured state improves coherence. (2) **Phase 2 (post-publication)**: Use autoresearch loop to optimize tensor format and Haiku rules. Autoresearch does the heavy lifting of hyperparameter search (strand structure, promotion/eviction thresholds, claim density). (3) **Result of Phase 2**: A published follow-up ('Optimizing Epistemic Projection: Hyperparameter Search for Bounded Cognitive Processing') showing how to tune Hamutay for specific reasoning tasks. Key decision: Autoresearch should NOT be in the critical path for Phase 1. Phase 1 aims to validate H0 with a reasonable (not optimal) tensor configuration. Once Phase 1 is published, Phase 2 can explore autoresearch. This keeps Phase 1 timeline realistic (4-6 weeks) and focuses effort on the core research question (does epistemic projection help?). Integration point: Autoresearch loop could run tensorfield evaluations (standardized micro-benchmarks) against live Hamutay pipeline. Each evaluation produces metrics (coherence, token efficiency, reasoning depth). Autoresearch learns which tensor configurations maximize these metrics. This is a natural follow-on project.

key_claims.text: Autoresearch loop could be used as meta-level tool to explore tensor configuration space (strand count, claim density, epistemic thresholds) once Hamutay pipeline is working.

Autoresearch should NOT be in critical path for Phase 1 (H0 validation). Phase 1 uses fixed, reasonable tensor config. Phase 2 (post-publication) uses autoresearch to optimize. Keeps Phase 1 timeline realistic.
Integration: autoresearch runs tensorfield micro-benchmarks against live Hamutay. Learns which configs maximize coherence, token efficiency, reasoning depth. Natural follow-on project after Phase 1.

strands.title: Hot Mess Paper Analysis: Context Length as Cognitive Load (New Strand, Cycle 25)
strands.content: CYCLE 25 CRITICAL INPUT: User flags 'Context Length Alone Hurts LLM Performance Despite Perfect Retrieval' (2025, hot mess paper) as directly relevant. This paper is a **direct validation source for H0 itself**. Key findings from paper (pending full read, but anticipated): (1) **Context length degrades performance even with perfect information retrieval.** This means the problem is not 'I can't find relevant info' but 'I can't reason coherently when context gets large.' This is exactly the cognitive load signal H0 predicts. (2) **Implication for H0**: If context length alone causes coherence degradation, then structured epistemic projection (which compresses context into semantic structure) should mitigate this degradation. Hamutay is a concrete instantiation of this mitigation. (3) **Paper as validation material**: Once H0 experiment is run, comparing results to hot mess paper findings will strengthen the publication. Result: 'We address the hot mess paper's finding by proposing epistemic projection. Unlike just adding retrieval (which doesn't help), we compress context into structured state. Here's evidence that it works.' (4) **Paper as motivation source**: The hot mess paper provides strong motivation for why H0 matters. If context length is inherently toxic to reasoning, then novel architectures (like Hamutay) are necessary, not optional. This makes the motivation section of the paper stronger. Action: User should either (a) read the paper directly and summarize findings, or (b) we use an agent to summarize it. Either way, the paper's key insights should be integrated into Cycle 25 tensor before engineering sprint begins. This paper is too relevant to skip.

key_claims.text: Hot mess paper: context length alone degrades reasoning performance even with perfect retrieval. Problem is not information access but reasoning coherence under cognitive load.

H0 directly addresses hot mess paper: epistemic projection (structured semantic compression) should mitigate context-length degradation. Unlike retrieval augmentation (ineffective), compression changes the computational structure.

Hot mess paper validates H0 motivation: context length is inherently toxic to reasoning. Therefore, novel architectures (Hamutay with epistemic projection) are necessary, not incremental improvements.

Integration plan: read/summarize hot mess paper by end of Cycle 25. Use findings as (a) validation source for H0 experiment results, (b) motivation source for H0 research narrative, (c) baseline for comparison (if hot mess found X degradation, does Hamutay reduce it?).

strands.title: Implementation Strategy: Build the Pipeline from Scratch for H0 Validation
strands.content: CYCLE 25 ENGINEERING FOCUS: Do NOT pre-construct tensors from historical conversations. Build pipeline from scratch and run it fresh on the Riemann hypothesis task. This remains locked from Cycle 24. Cycle 25 adds concrete implementation entry point: start with the controller loop skeleton. The loop does: (1) fetch tensor_durable + tensor_ephemeral from state store (Apacheta format), (2) decode them into context string, (3) call transformer with `<system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>` framing, (4) writeback: parse tensor' from output, apply Haiku rules (promote/evict), store updated state. This loop is minimal, deterministic, and provider-independent. Implementation order: (1) **Controller loop skeleton** (fetch-decode-execute-writeback), (2) **Haiku memory rules** (concrete thresholds, edge cases), (3) **ALU prompt v0** (transformer instruction + tensor' format), (4) **Test harness** (condition A/B/C orchestration), (5) **Riemann experiment run** (collect outputs, embed, measure variance). Two implementations: primary (OpenRouter + OLMo-3) for cost/speed, secondary (Anthropic API + caching) for quality/feature exploration. No changes to strategy from Cycle 24. Engineering sprint can begin once specs lock (end of Cycle 25).

key_claims.text: Do NOT pre-construct tensors from historical data. Build pipeline from scratch and run it on Riemann task live. Avoids circularity and tests actual H0 (structured state improves cognition in real time).
Pipeline validation subsumes tensor quality. If pipeline produces incoherent tensors, that's valid research result: evidence that architecture needs redesign or is fundamentally harder than expected.
Honest H0 test: 'Can a model maintaining structured state produce more coherent cognition than one without?' Answer comes from live pipeline performance, not post-hoc tensor extraction.

Implementation workflow: build Hamutay (native first) → implement Haiku (deterministic rules) → write ALU prompt → baseline condition setup → run experiment → embed+measure variance → publish results (success or failure are both valid).

Controller loop entry point: fetch tensors → decode to context → call transformer → writeback (Haiku rules) → store. This minimal loop is the bootstrap point for engineering. Provider-independent (works with Anthropic, OpenRouter, local).

strands.title: Research Output: From Implementation Success to Publishable Results
strands.content: CYCLE 25 PUBLICATION STRATEGY UNCHANGED: Two possible outcomes, both publishable. **OUTCOME A: Pipeline works. H0 is supported.** Hamutay produces coherent tensors. Baseline condition shows high variance in Riemann proof attempts. Structured condition shows low variance. Publication: 'Hamutay: Epistemic Projection for Bounded Cognitive Processing' (ICLR/ICML or NeurIPS workshop, or arXiv). **OUTCOME B: Pipeline doesn't work. H0 is not supported.** Hamutay struggles. Structured condition shows similar variance to baseline, or worse. Publication: 'Why Bounded Cognitive Projection Fails' (arXiv or ICLR workshop). Both are peer-reviewable, valid research. Cycle 25 adds motivation source (hot mess paper) to strengthen either outcome. If Outcome A: 'We address context-length degradation (hot mess paper) by proposing epistemic projection.' If Outcome B: 'We attempted epistemic projection as a solution to hot mess paper findings; here's why it didn't work.' Timeline: 4-6 weeks engineering + 2-3 weeks writing. Realistic for 2-3 person team.

key_claims.text: Outcome A (pipeline works): publish architecture success paper with H0 validation via variance decomposition (coherent vs. chaotic failure on Riemann task).
Outcome B (pipeline doesn't work): publish failure analysis paper documenting challenges in prompt-based structured output, tensor stability, memory control. Valid research contribution.
Both outcomes are publishable. H0 support = architecture validation. H0 failure = design lesson for next iteration. Commitment is to run experiment honestly and publish results transparently.

Hot mess paper as motivation source: context-length degradation is documented problem. H0 offers novel solution (epistemic projection). Either outcome (success or failure) directly addresses motivating research gap.

strands.title: GPU Compute and Model Choice: Practical Constraints and Decisions
strands.content: CYCLE 25 UNCHANGED: Primary implementation on OLMo-3 + OpenRouter. Cost-controlled, provider-agnostic, transparent, reproducible. Secondary validation on Claude Sonnet. Compute budget: ~$100-150 for full experiment. Not a blocker. Cycle 25 adds clarity: with autoresearch as Phase 2 post-publication project, compute budget for Phase 1 remains modest. Phase 2 (autoresearch hyperparameter search) will require more compute, but that's deferred and funded separately. Phase 1 stays lean.

key_claims.text: Primary implementation on OLMo-3 + OpenRouter: cost-controlled, provider-agnostic, transparent, reproducible. Sufficient for H0 validation (all models will fail on Riemann).
Secondary validation on Claude Sonnet tests generalization to frontier models. Together, OLMo-3 + Sonnet provide breadth without frontier-model-only requirement.
Compute budget: ~$100-150 for full experiment (45 runs, 225-450k tokens, plus validation). Not a resource blocker. Autoresearch (Phase 2) budgeted separately post-publication.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed specification of concrete Haiku promotion/eviction thresholds (T > 0.8, N=2, size limits)
declared_losses.why: Deferred to implementation phase where empirical validation is possible. Cycle 25 affirms structure from Cycle 24 but defers numerical tuning.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed transformer prompt template (exact instruction phrases for ALU role, tensor' format, epistemic guidance, STOP condition)
declared_losses.why: Version-0 prompts will be iterated during implementation. Deferred to engineering sprint where prompt engineering feedback loop is active.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed specification of baseline condition (exact prompt, context length, reasoning steps to match historical patterns)
declared_losses.why: Baseline structure is clear (vanilla append-only, no tensor machinery), but specifics depend on Hamutay choices and hot mess paper analysis. Deferred to engineering sprint.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Specification of embedding model for variance analysis (sentence-BERT? other?), embedding dimensionality, distance metric, clustering method
declared_losses.why: Technical detail deferred to implementation. Multiple valid choices; decision can await engineering phase.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Definition of 'coherence' in the context of guaranteed failure (what does it mean for two Riemann proof attempts to be 'coherent' with each other?)
declared_losses.why: Operational definition is 'lower pairwise distance in embedding space = higher coherence.' Assumption (semantically similar wrong approaches cluster) should be validated empirically. Deferred to experiment analysis.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Full summary of hot mess paper findings and implications
declared_losses.why: Paper flagged in Cycle 25 as critical input. User indicated paper should be read directly or summarized by agent. Paper summary not yet available. This is an open action item, not a completed loss. Will be integrated once read.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed technical roadmap for integrating autoresearch loop into Phase 2 optimization (how exactly does autoresearch interface with Hamutay? what are tensorfield benchmarks?)
declared_losses.why: Autoresearch is Phase 2 post-publication project. Integrating it in detail now would distract from Phase 1 (H0 validation). Deferred to Phase 2 planning once Phase 1 results are in hand.

open_questions: What is the expected runtime for a single Riemann proof attempt under each condition? How many transformer calls (Hamutay cycles) before a model signals STOP or times out?
Should baseline condition enforce a hard token limit (to simulate context window pressure), or should it be allowed to grow indefinitely (to separate 'token depletion' from 'reasoning incoherence')?
How many attempts (K) are needed for embedding variance to be statistically meaningful? Is K=10-15 sufficient, or should it be higher (K=25-30)?

What is the prior expectation for baseline variance? If we assume vanilla append-only produces 'hot mess,' what does 'hot mess' look like quantitatively in embedding space? What baseline dispersion value would count as evidence of success for structured condition?
Should the transformer be told explicitly that the Riemann hypothesis is unproven, or should it be left to discover that during reasoning? Does this affect the variance measurement?

How should Haiku handle the edge case where the transformer signals STOP but the reasoning is clearly incomplete (e.g., stopped after 1-2 cycles)? Is early STOP a sign of good epistemic judgment, or a sign of failure?

Should the experiment include a **fourth condition**: structured input + explicitly disabled STOP (force transformer to continue reasoning until hard token limit)? Would this isolate the STOP signal's contribution to coherence?
How should declared_losses be handled in tensor' when the transformer can't produce reliable structured output? Should Haiku infer losses from omissions, or only record explicit losses?

Should the pipeline include logging/tracing so that you can post-hoc analyze *which* strands and key_claims were promoted/evicted during the Riemann attempt? This would enable analysis of whether evictions degraded final coherence.

What constitutes 'success' for the embedding variance measurement? Is tighter clustering in structured condition sufficient, or do you need correlation with other metrics (e.g., reasoning depth, STOP timing)?
Should you re-run the experiment with different models (not just OLMo-3 + Sonnet, but also others) to test H0 generality, or is 2-model validation sufficient for initial publication?

If structured condition shows dramatically lower variance but also lower absolute quality (more clearly wrong), how should this be interpreted? Coherent failure vs. baseline chaos — which is better for the research narrative?

Should the paper include a section on 'failure modes we encountered'? If Hamutay breaks during implementation (e.g., transformer can't reliably produce tensor'), how should this be handled in publication?

What is the plan for artifact release? Should you publish the trained tensors, prompt templates, embedding analysis code, and full experimental logs? This would maximize reproducibility but also complexity.
Should this experiment feed back into Yanantin schema refinement, or is Yanantin treated as immutable for this project?

**NEW (Cycle 25)**: What are the key findings from the hot mess paper? How exactly does context length degrade performance? Is it token count, information density, or something else? How should this inform baseline condition design?

**NEW (Cycle 25)**: How should autoresearch loop interface with Hamutay? What would tensorfield micro-benchmarks look like? Should Phase 2 autoresearch use the same Riemann task or different evaluation tasks?

**NEW (Cycle 25)**: Should we run a quick proof-of-concept (single Riemann attempt, Hamutay pipeline v0, crude tensor projection) before committing to full experiment? Risk: delays pipeline completion. Benefit: catches fundamental blockers early.

**NEW (Cycle 25)**: The controller loop is minimal (fetch-decode-execute-writeback). Should we build it first as a 'hello world' before implementing Haiku and ALU prompt? This would validate architecture at the skeleton level.

**NEW (Cycle 25)**: Should the two implementations (OpenRouter + Anthropic API) run on the same underlying code (single codebase with provider abstraction layer) or separate codebases? Tradeoff: code reuse vs. simplicity per implementation.

Lock by end of Cycle 26. This spec feeds into test harness implementation.",

Recommend: sentence-BERT for simplicity, cosine distance for standard practice, no explicit clustering (just pairwise distances for dispersion). Lock by end of Cycle 26. This decision simplifies test harness implementation (don't need complex clustering libraries).",

Test manually on OLMo-3 to ensure it produces a coherent proof attempt (even if wrong). Lock by end of Cycle 26.",

Write 1-page Phase 2 plan. This clarifies scope boundary between Phase 1 and Phase 2, preventing Phase 1 scope creep.",

This skeleton is implementation-agnostic; it defines the evaluation protocol. Lock by end of Cycle 26.",

(3) **ALU prompt**: transformer doesn't produce valid JSON, doesn't follow instructions. Mitigation: test v0 prompts now, iterate. (4) **Embedding variance**: all conditions cluster similarly (inconclusive). Mitigation: this is a valid result; document why variance didn't discriminate. Suggest tweaks to hypothesis? (2) **How does it inform baseline condition?** Should baseline enforce token limits? Vary context length? (3) **How does it provide comparison baseline?** If hot mess found X% performance degradation at context length Y, does Hamutay reduce that? By how much would be 'success'?
